speaker: interviewee
text: Came at the right time, or past the right time.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two of an interview with Alex Stern, February 28th, 1996. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Alex, can you tell me what was the reaction of non-Jews to what was going on to the Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would say mostly they were very happy about it. They didn't like us too much to begin with, so it gave them a chance to show their hatred. And it was a Christian town, and in those years, Christianity was against whatever Hebrew study was, and they were mostly happy. And those real anti-Semites were taking advantage on it. School was getting tough. I remember my little sister once came home and a boy was running behind her and tried to hit her, and I was on my bike and I took my pump, and I hit him in the head. I don't know what happened to him, and I was hiding in the house for days. I was afraid to go out. But I wouldn't let him hurt my little sister. So it was-- it was very hard. It was hard on everybody. Nobody really mentioned the hardship to not be able to go to do business, the hardship of-- of surviving. So it was getting really bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any requirements of the Jewish men to-- to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely, absolutely, according to the age. When they were in an age of [INAUDIBLE], they took them to forced labor out of town. At that time I was younger. They put us to work in town, or watching at night that nobody lets light out of the window. If anything, we had to report it. So it was called, like, a service to the community, but being Jews, we had to stay up and watch the town or did whatever was necessary to do. We had hard labor no matter what we did. We had to do something. Otherwise, they found work for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of the men taken from the town for labor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. My brother, my oldest brother, and uncles, they left as soon as the Hungarians came in. I mean, instead of taking them to the army at the age of military, they took them to work all over the country and even out of the country. So only one of my brothers left because he was at that age that he had to go. But my uncles, they took them all away. They went to work. It was tough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear from them? Did they keep in touch?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we did hear from them. They were able to write. And I had one uncle who was in Ukraine with his brother. That was my aunt's husband. There was typhus in the camp, and they put everybody in that barrack and burned it. So somehow word got around in town what they did. That's the saddest thing we heard about. There were a few from our town who perished in that fire. They couldn't-- it was-- they were afraid it's going to infect the whole area so they just burned down the whole thing. There were over 1,000 people involved in this. Somehow it got word to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Did you hear news of what was going on in the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: The way they were writing this-- we had a paper. We had a paper from before. It came every day, and they were always winning. So if somebody wanted to know what's really going on, if they have a radio, and they were lucky at midnight, they could catch London, if not.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say in the paper it said they were always winning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Always winning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is the they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, let's say, for instance, in-- in Africa, [INAUDIBLE] was in Africa. So the Hungarian used to write like a Roman avoids. He-- he went out of the way or something like that. Not that he's losing, or not that he was beaten back by the Americans and English, but he was trying to avoid something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So which country was always winning?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans, of course, The Axis. And in the beginning, they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you hear anything about the extermination camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in the beginning at all. They only thing that we really heard about that they considered a necessity, because they were afraid that the sickness is going to spread. But really, not much about in  those years.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find out that you are going to be-- to have to leave your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was in '44, in the beginning of '44. They were already picking up Jews from other towns. And you know, they always went around, and they were picking up Jews, town by town. And they take them all to the big city. So when our turn came, I remember I was in the vineyard.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a Christian up there-- who, you know, we were very close. We saw him every day. So he said to me, if you go home, they're gonna take you to the school because they called the Jews to the school. So like, I had second thoughts. Should I go home? Where else could I have gone? So  I went home. I went with my family. Did your family know where they were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely not. Nobody did. That was a systematic work. They picked up people, and they took them, and nobody knows where. They told you, in case you're going to work somewhere, the men are going to work and the women are going to take care of them. First of all, you weren't even allowed to ask. They just told you go, and you have to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you able to take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to begin with, they told you, you want to take what you are able to carry. People always have their favorite items they want to carry with them. And everybody loaded up as you could. And you had to carry all that stuff. And we have to get rid of it anyway. But we tried to carry as much as we could.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mother able to keep her jewelry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, of course-- of course. They didn't tell you to take your jewelry off. She carried  it until the time came, and they took everything away from us anyway. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you taken at first?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were taken to [NON-ENGLISH]. That's the name they called the city where the big ghetto was. And [NON-ENGLISH] was a big Jewish community. And from all around [NON-ENGLISH] the whole area was taken. We lived about an hour ride from [NON-ENGLISH]. So they took us there and get together everybody from all over the area. So many people, there was one on top of the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much of your family was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, my oldest brother went to work. And there was my father, my mother, and my two sisters. My second brother was somewhere else, and he finally came with us together. He wanted to be with us. So we thought maybe he escaped or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother went with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH], my second brother. And he came, and met there in [NON-ENGLISH]. Then, my mother wasn't too happy about it. She said, I hope you escape somehow. He came because he wanted to be together with the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the family housed when you got to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a-- you would call it a soldier's-- not only barracks. It was a big, big place. There were all  kinds of buildings and stables. And we wound up in a building near the stables. And there were people in the rooms, under the stairs, under-- wherever there was room to lay down, we were put up. And who came after us, there was no roof on their top. They just put them in the middle of the street. Like, they put down whatever they had, and there they would be camped.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many Jewish people were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd say  about 30,000 in [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, did you have an apartment, or a room?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Over there, they just put everybody one on top of the other, wherever they fit, wherever the came, and they would stand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family, where did--

speaker: interviewee
text: My family, my parents were in a-- it's like a staircase, but it was a big space there. And we got that space there. And we slept there. But we were there for a short time only. And I remember that, when they got together the people from the smaller towns, and they were down on the street, I saw my aunt with her two children. Of course, her husband was in a forced labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your aunt's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt's name, was-- the one that lived in [NON-ENGLISH]. And the other one was [NON-ENGLISH]. And the one was [NON-ENGLISH]. And she had two children. And [NON-ENGLISH] lived in [NON-ENGLISH]. She also had two children. And they were there outside. And I used to go and visit them and try to help them out because their husbands were in working camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get food and water?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, we did carry food with us. So they really didn't have no way to cook there or prepare there, but anyone had enough food to last them for at least a week. Now, the one thing I remember, when they moved the people who were on the street, they left everything else there. And we got up in the morning, and we only saw the bundles there. So we knew they took them somewhere. And a lot of people who were poor or needed some clothes or shoes or food, they were looking over. So it's like going through those people's belongings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what happened to the people who belonged to the bundles?

speaker: interviewee
text: No way-- no way. But that was one of the clues that we were getting that--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you saw the bundles where your aunt had been.

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible. My aunt had two little children, and they're just gone in the middle of the night. And we didn't even know when it was. It was so quiet, and they just took them out of there, and that was the end of it. And later on, we found out when they took out of there and they took us to the train station. Then, we knew  where they went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father able to work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nobody worked there. We were just waiting for things to happen. So they didn't put nobody there. There were so many people there, there wasn't enough room to turn around. So it was a concentration of people there  in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not in our-- not in our-- It wasn't called a Judenrat, even if there were. But there was the Hungarian gendarmes, as they called them in them in those years. They took care of the inside of-- of the ghetto, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they treat the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: They treated like-- like-- like prisoners. Like, they cut their beards off, and they-- they did the worse things they could. There was no-- no control. It was Antisemitism. And they cut my father's beard off, they cut the Rabbi's beard off. And it started panic. It started people to see that this-- this was coming to an end. So everybody lost like, like control. I mean, nobody was able to-- to think or to do anything about it, so-- And it was such big guarding in-- in the ghetto that you couldn't go out of the ghetto, no way. Nobody even tried.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when this was happening, when you saw your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Terrible. Terrible. I know my father, since I had my mind to use, he always had a long beard. And suddenly they cut his beard off. I thought half of his face disappeared. It was terrible-- it was terrible. Those days were like-- like you felt like-- like-- like everything-- the ground went out underneath you. So it was awful-- it was awful. If you start thinking back, it's just-- you get dizzy. And it was unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you taken next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to the train station. They put us in cattle trains. They-- they used to put people in, and when it was full already, they-- they-- they pushed in 10 or 20 more. So sometimes, you were afraid the-- the-- the-- the wagon is going to bust, so many people they pushed in  in one wagon. It was terrible. Luckily, I was able to get one of those small window that  they had, iron bars. And, you know, when the train was moving, we had a little air coming in. But inside, that's smell overnight, and people crying and yelling. And I remember one particular lady, they lived on the other side, on the Romanian side on Transylvania. And they came to visit their parents in our side. And she was crying and hollering at her parents that she should have gone back, and why did she have to come here. Their family is still over there, where they were from before. And it was awful. Things, you know, you remember suddenly, but it was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you food and water on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] On the train, nothing. We still had something, what we carried with us, but not much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you allowed to bring your belongings with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our belongings stayed in the ghetto. We couldn't carry nothing when we left the ghetto. So everything, what it was, in bundles. Like a little food you were able  to put in your pockets or whatever they didn't see, so we're able, for the next few days, to survive. But there was no food. There was no food in the wagons. Whatever we took with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family still together in the wagon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not all of them, no. There were more-- I remember my grandmother was with us, and my parents, of course, and brother and sister, we stuck together. But you see, when you put together our town-- it was a small town-- and you mix them in-- in 30,000 people, or whoever they pushed in the wagon, you were lucky you knew somebody in that wagon.

speaker: interviewee
text: So everybody looked liked from another world. The only thing I remember was my grandma, and she was sick, and she was crying. And, of course, the parents, they stick together. That's all. What happened when the train stopped?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the train stopped quite a few times. But they only opened the door once a day. And mostly at night, they were able to take out whatever dirt there was to take away some of the smell. So they allowed one or two men to get-- empty that-- you could call it a big, empty pot, whatever. And that's about all, once a day, and that's it. It was awful. It's like you lost your mind when you were in there. You think back that this ever happened, or what happened in that wagon, it's-- it's a very rough question because it was something that-- you lost control of time. You've just gotten to somewhere that you-- you hoped never existed. So-- But it did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see when you reached your destination?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they did open the doors. And there were a lot of people there that we never saw, we never heard. When they opened the door, they started hollering in all kinds of languages-- like Polish, Russian, German-- and everybody was hollering, run, run, run, run, and with sticks in their hands. We arrived in Birkenau at night. There were those big lights from the top, and they were hitting and-- move, move, move, move. And you went like-- pushed by the rest of the people. And suddenly, they separated you. They told you, you go here, you go there. And if you didn't go, they hit you. And you heard barking dogs running there. It was just something that, today, you would say it's from the You wouldn't believe things like this could ever exist or happen. It was not humane. It was terrible-- terrible. You don't treat animals like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got out from the wagons. And after that, you know, like-- like people pushed each other. You know, you just-- you just-- You didn't even have to walk. They carried you wherever they wanted you to go, so. It's terrible. What happened to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as far as I remember, somehow, we got so separated, right when we jumped out of the wagon. I remember my brother-- I mentioned before, [NON-ENGLISH], and my father, only three of us were able to get together after they emptied the wagon. My mother and sisters, I haven't seen them since. Somehow, that particular moment was so tough, so running, so pushing. And [SIGHS] the only thing we could do-- the had the men separated. And I was looking, and I found my father and brother. And that's how we stuck together, stuck together, the three of us. But once over there, arriving there were people from all over the world. You didn't even know what in the world is happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see where your mother went?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not. A lot of [SIGHS] people claimed they saw her. I was just-- I'd say, I was carried out of the wagon, and it just disappeared-- everybody disappeared. And at once you find yourself between people from another wagon and from another world, and that's the way I felt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not exactly, at the time. I mean, today everybody knows where we were. We just were pushed out and push, and push, and holler, and go, go, go, go-- in German, of course. I didn't. I'm very honest about it. At that time, somehow, like-- like-- like, I couldn't believe it and I couldn't think about it. That's the way I felt.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you and your brother and father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- my brother and my father and myself, we-- they took us from there. They cut our hair. They took our clothes off. They looked into our mouth. They looked in everywhere in our body. And then we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they looking for? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever you had on you. Is it jewelry, or is it-- whatever it could have been, they took it away from you. You went in there, you had nothing but your body-- nothing but your body. That's all you had. And I stuck to my brother and father. That's the only thing I was close, after this happened. All people from all over, I never knew nobody, and  I never even could imagine where they were coming from. So that's the way things were happening [? in a hurry. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a registration?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that came later, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get a number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, sure I got a number. I got a number as soon as we took a shower, they cut our hair off, they got us some spray under-- under our arms and everywhere else. They called it the [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] fertilizer, whatever. And that's all we got. And then we were taken into another line. And then they start asking your name, and where you're from. And after that, they took us to another place where they put a number on our arm. So [SIGHS] things happened so fast. And you were so dazed, even to start analyzing.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find out where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were inside, of course. We started to talk. There were Polish Jews, French Jews. And we found out there even German people. They were, you see, when you wear your-- your numbers on your-- on your jacket, if you were a Jew, you had the yellow color. If you were a political inmate, you had a green color. If you were a criminal inmate, you had a red color. So there were all kinds of-- not only Jews there, who'd we spent already in there, in the camps. There were all kinds of nationalities, Russians, Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz-- Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happened in Auschwitz was that we were the three of us together. And I was very, very close to my brother. He was one year older than I was. And I always considered him a smart boy. And I wanted to be with him, together. And just one morning, we stood in line there, they called his name.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when they took him out, I ran after him. And I got so beaten up that I couldn't get up. So I stayed with my father. And my father was a very heavy smoker. And when we got into the camp, he suffered very much. He was a chain smoker. He smoked one cigarette after the other.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was afraid he was losing his mind. He couldn't get a cigarette. And once, I remember we were standing in line, and a German threw a cigarette end, and they were all jumping after it. And none of them got nothing of it. And the German was laughing. And so I saw that I'm not going to survive this, because it was killing me, seeing  my father in such an agony. And the first time they were looking for builders. So I got up and I said I'm a builder. So they took me away. And my father stayed  there in the camp. And I went, at that time-- they took me to [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a chance to say goodbye to your father before you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't even have a chance to look back. Once they called your name, they made sure that you run, not walk-- you run. So that's the way life was there. That's the way I remember it. My father, [SIGHS] may he rest in peace, he didn't come home. He must have gone somewhere else, or he maybe could have died in Auschwitz. I don't know where he died, but I know he never came home. That's what [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was the destiny, I guess. We-- we didn't know what's going to happen, but it did happen. Today we do know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it's a thing that, today, you talk about civilized people, Germans and all kind of-- they invent everything else, and they invent how to kill people. And that's something that I could never live with, and I still can't live with it. So at that time, you wouldn't believe it, but it was true. It was just plain terrible. I think my biggest agony was seeing my father under those circumstances. It was just terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I couldn't live with it. So I try to get away from that.
